what_it_does: Defines CSS presentation styles for a tonal zoning design-system handoff page, including document layout, masthead, section scaffolding, zone registry cards, component-role demos, an applied overview dashboard mock, Vi rail styling, spatial overlays, and legend elements.
observations: Uses CSS custom properties extensively for zone surfaces, edges, ink, typography, radius, shadows, spacing-adjacent values, and motion timing.
Adds a fixed faint architectural grid over the body background with masked radial fade and pointer-events disabled.
Builds a zone registry grid where each card shows surface, strong chip, ink dot, metadata, and token rows.
Defines local component vocabulary for buttons, chips, and a Vi badge using zone role variables.
Includes a large dashboard mock with left navigation, main canvas, zoned blocks, stats, tiles, activity rows, right Vi rail, ghost side panels, and spatial flow layers.
Several UI surfaces use literal white backgrounds and one literal rgba shadow alongside tokenized colors.
standouts: The file treats tonal zones as composable visual roles rather than single-use component colors.
The applied dashboard mock demonstrates multiple zone roles in one composed interface.
Spatial context is expressed through grid overlays, ghost panels, and flow layers rather than only card styling.
conventions: Class names are prefixed mainly with tz-, ov-, and cvx- to separate document, overview mock, and shared demo component scopes.
Sections are organized with large comment banners and smaller inline comments.
Zone styling follows surface, strong, edge, and ink variable pairings.
Typography is assigned through font shorthand declarations using design-token font variables.
Interactive emphasis is minimal, with hover lift on registry cards and cursor styling on button-like elements.
concerns: The opening comment says the file does not invent colour, but the CSS includes literal #fff backgrounds and an rgba shadow value.
The overview mock has a fixed width of 1240px and fixed grid columns, so responsiveness depends on external scaling or containment not shown here.
Some selectors are highly presentation-specific and tightly coupled to the mock structure, such as nested ov-rail and tile internals.
The declaration '.ov-row' sets both border-left and border afterward, which means the later border shorthand can override the earlier left-border width/style intent.
notes: This file is the presentation layer that makes the tonal-zone registry and its applied dashboard example visible as a designed handoff page.
questions: Where are the zone custom properties and typography tokens defined?
Is the fixed-width overview mock intended to be scaled by script or surrounding CSS outside this file?
Should literal white and rgba values be replaced with tokens to match the stated no-new-colour rule?
Are the spatial flow layers populated by markup or script elsewhere?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose mostly matches: it composes zone roles into deliverable presentation styles, though the file also contains a few literal color values despite saying it does not invent colour.
intention_signals: It seems intended to make abstract tonal-zone roles legible by showing them first as a registry, then as reusable component states, and finally as a realistic dashboard composition.
novelty: The file combines a design-token zone registry with a fully styled applied product mock in the same stylesheet, including spatial continuation cues and a dedicated Vi assistance rail.
contribution: Provides a neutral presentation vocabulary for tonal zoning: zone cards, role demos, applied dashboard regions, spatial overlays, legends, and assistant-rail treatments that a fused system could retain as demonstrative interface patterns.
summary_for_embedding: CSS presentation layer for a tonal zoning design-system handoff page with token-based zone cards, component-role demos, dashboard mock styling, spatial overlays, and legend scaffolding.
intention_for_embedding: This was for demonstrating how tonal surface-zone roles compose across documentation, reusable UI states, and an applied dashboard interface.
